sections.heading: What it is
sections.html: The model's short-term attention: the context window. Every turn you see here is re-sent to the LLM verbatim, on every single request. Nothing here is "learned" - it is literally pasted into the prompt each time.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.html: LLMs are stateless. Between two API calls the model remembers nothing. The illusion of a continuous conversation is created by replaying this buffer.

sections.heading: Its fatal flaw
sections.html: It is finite (watch the token bar fill) and volatile - close the session and it is gone. That is exactly why the other three memory systems exist: they are the agent's way of saving what matters before this buffer dies.

sections.heading: Try it
sections.html: Press "end session" in the top bar and watch the buffer get compressed into a single episodic memory before being wiped.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.html: A diary of events: time-stamped records of what happened in each exchange - who said what, when, and how important it felt. Like your own memory of "that conversation last Tuesday".

sections.heading: How it gets written
sections.html: After every reply, the extraction stage summarizes the exchange into one episode. When a session ends, the whole conversation is consolidated into a single richer episode - that mirrors what happens in humans during sleep.

sections.heading: How it gets read
sections.html: When a new message resembles a past event, retrieval pulls the episode into contextual memory, so the agent can say "last time we talked about X...".

sections.heading: Episodic vs semantic
sections.html: Episodic remembers that Tuesday you mentioned Rust. Semantic remembers you are learning Rust - the event stripped away, only the knowledge kept.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.html: Distilled knowledge about you: stable facts (name, job, location), preferences (tastes, working style), and skills (what you know or are learning). The original sentence is thrown away; only the meaning survives.

sections.heading: How it gets written
sections.html: Stage 5 of the pipeline runs an extraction pass over every exchange asking: "is there anything durable and reusable here?". Chit-chat is rejected; identity, preference and capability statements are kept, each with a recorded reason.

sections.heading: How it gets updated
sections.html: New statements that contradict or refine an old memory update it in place instead of duplicating it - say "actually I prefer long answers now" and watch the preference item change, keeping its full edit history.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.html: This store is what makes an agent feel like it knows you across sessions. It survives "end session" and even a browser restart (it lives in localStorage).

sections.heading: What it is
sections.html: Not a store - a spotlight. For every message you send, the agent searches its episodic + semantic stores, scores every memory for relevance, and injects only the winners into the prompt. This panel shows exactly what was recalled for the last message, and why.

sections.heading: How scoring works here
sections.html: This demo uses transparent lexical scoring: word overlap between your message and each memory, normalized by memory length, plus a recency boost, a reinforcement boost (often-recalled memories score higher), and an importance boost. Production systems use embedding vectors instead - same idea, fuzzier matching.

sections.heading: Why not inject everything?
sections.html: The context window is small and expensive (watch the blue token bar). Retrieval is the art of spending those tokens only on memories that will actually improve this specific answer.

sections.heading: See it yourself
sections.html: Each retrieved card shows its score, which of your words matched, and glows teal in its home panel at the moment of recall. Press "prompt x-ray" to see the retrieved memories sitting inside the real prompt.
